title: Brain Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for brain anatomy, structures, and locations

questions.q: The cerebrum is divided into left and right halves called:
questions.explain: The cerebrum consists of two hemispheres — left and right — connected by the corpus callosum. Each hemisphere controls the opposite side of the body.

optionExplains.text: Lobes are subdivisions WITHIN each hemisphere — frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital. Not the main division.
Hemispheres — the two great halves of the cerebrum. Left hemisphere typically dominates language; right dominates spatial processing. The corpus callosum bridges them.
Ventricles are fluid-filled cavities inside the brain, not divisions of the cerebrum.
Gyri are the ridges on the brain surface — the bumps between grooves. Not the major divisions.

questions.q: The shallow grooves on the brain surface are called:
questions.explain: Sulci (singular: Sulcus) are the shallow grooves between brain folds. They increase surface area without increasing skull size.

optionExplains.text: Fissures are DEEP grooves — like the longitudinal fissure between hemispheres. Sulci are shallower.
Gyri are the ridges — the bumps between grooves. Opposite of sulci.
Sulci are shallow grooves separating gyri. The central sulcus separates frontal from parietal lobes. The lateral sulcus marks the temporal lobe boundary.
Ventricles are internal fluid spaces, not surface grooves.

questions.q: Which lobe of the cerebrum is primarily responsible for vision?
questions.explain: The occipital lobe at the back of the brain processes visual information. Damage here causes cortical blindness even with healthy eyes.

optionExplains.text: Frontal lobe handles motor control, planning, personality. Not primary vision.
Parietal lobe processes sensory information — touch, spatial awareness. Not vision.
Temporal lobe handles hearing, memory, language. Some visual processing but not primary.
Occipital lobe — the visual cortex lives here. Primary visual cortex (V1) receives input from the eyes; surrounding areas process edges, motion, color, faces.

questions.q: The brainstem consists of the midbrain, pons, and:
questions.explain: The brainstem is three parts: Midbrain (superior), pons (middle), and medulla oblongata (inferior). It connects brain to spinal cord and controls vital functions.

optionExplains.text: Thalamus is part of the diencephalon, not the brainstem. It's the sensory relay station.
Hypothalamus is also diencephalon — controls homeostasis, not brainstem tissue.
Medulla oblongata — the brainstem's inferior portion, connecting to the spinal cord. Controls heart rate, breathing, blood pressure. Life depends on it.
Cerebellum is posterior to the brainstem, not part of it. It coordinates movement separately.

questions.q: The corpus callosum is composed of:
questions.explain: The corpus callosum is the largest white matter tract in the brain — myelinated axons connecting left and right hemispheres.

optionExplains.text: Gray matter contains cell bodies. The corpus callosum is axons — myelinated fibers.
White matter — hundreds of millions of myelinated axons forming an information highway between hemispheres. Cut it and each hemisphere becomes an island.
CSF fills ventricles and surrounds the brain. The corpus callosum is solid neural tissue.
Meninges are protective membranes, not inter-hemispheric connections.

questions.q: The primary motor cortex is located in which gyrus?
questions.explain: The precentral gyrus contains the primary motor cortex — immediately anterior to the central sulcus. 'Pre' means before/in front of.

optionExplains.text: Postcentral gyrus is primary SOMATOSENSORY cortex — sensation, not movement. Behind the central sulcus.
Precentral gyrus — motor neurons here send commands down to muscles. The motor homunculus maps body parts along this strip. Hands and face get huge representation.
Superior temporal gyrus is primary auditory cortex — hearing, not movement.
Cingulate gyrus is part of the limbic system — emotion and attention, not primary motor control.

questions.q: The three protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord are called the:
questions.explain: The meninges are three membrane layers — dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater — protecting the CNS.

optionExplains.text: Ventricles are internal CSF-filled spaces, not protective membranes.
Meninges — dura (tough outer), arachnoid (middle web), pia (delicate inner). They cushion, protect, and contain cerebrospinal fluid. Meningitis is their inflammation.
Plexuses are networks — nerve plexuses or choroid plexuses. Not protective membranes.
Commissures are fiber tracts connecting brain regions, not protective membranes.

questions.q: Which ventricle is located within the diencephalon?
questions.explain: The third ventricle is a narrow slit between the left and right thalamus, within the diencephalon.

optionExplains.text: Lateral ventricles are within the cerebral hemispheres — one in each hemisphere.
Third ventricle — sandwiched between the two thalami in the diencephalon. It connects to lateral ventricles above via foramina and to fourth ventricle below via the cerebral aqueduct.
Fourth ventricle is in the hindbrain, between pons/medulla and cerebellum.
Central canal runs through the spinal cord, not the diencephalon.

questions.q: The thalamus functions primarily as a:
questions.explain: The thalamus relays sensory information to the cortex. Almost all sensory input passes through the thalamus before reaching conscious awareness.

optionExplains.text: Motor control involves basal ganglia, cerebellum, and motor cortex. Thalamus relays, not commands.
The thalamus is the sensory switchboard — visual, auditory, somatosensory information all route through here before reaching cortex. Only smell bypasses it directly.
Hormone production is hypothalamus and pituitary, not thalamus.
Memory involves hippocampus primarily. Thalamus relays information but doesn't store memories.

questions.q: The structure that coordinates voluntary muscle movements and maintains balance is the:
questions.explain: The cerebellum — 'little brain' — coordinates movements, maintains posture, and processes balance information. Damage causes ataxia.

optionExplains.text: Cerebrum initiates voluntary movement but doesn't fine-tune coordination. That's cerebellar.
Hypothalamus controls homeostasis — temperature, hunger, thirst. Not movement coordination.
Cerebellum — tucked posterior to the brainstem, it compares intended movements to actual movements and adjusts in real time. It's why you can touch your nose with eyes closed.
Medulla controls vital reflexes — breathing, heart rate. Not voluntary movement coordination.

questions.q: Broca's area is located in which lobe?
questions.explain: Broca's area is in the left frontal lobe (usually) and controls speech production. Damage causes Broca's aphasia — halting, effortful speech.

optionExplains.text: Parietal lobe handles sensory processing, not speech production.
Broca's area sits in the left inferior frontal gyrus. It plans the motor sequences for speaking. 'Broca's Broken Boca' — patients understand but can't produce fluent speech.
Temporal lobe contains Wernicke's area for language comprehension, not Broca's for production.
Occipital lobe is for vision. No language production there.

questions.q: The hippocampus is primarily involved in:
questions.explain: The hippocampus converts short-term memories into long-term memories. Damage prevents forming new memories (anterograde amnesia).

optionExplains.text: Motor coordination is cerebellar. Hippocampus is limbic — emotion and memory.
Hippocampus — shaped like a seahorse, essential for memory consolidation. H.M. Had his removed and couldn't form new memories, though old memories remained.
Visual processing is occipital. Hippocampus handles memory, including spatial memory.
Hormone secretion is hypothalamus and pituitary. Hippocampus is memory.

questions.q: The dura mater is the:
questions.explain: Dura mater means 'tough mother' — it's the thick, outermost meningeal layer attached to the skull's inner surface.

optionExplains.text: Pia mater is innermost — delicate and adherent to brain surface.
Arachnoid mater is middle — web-like, containing CSF in the subarachnoid space.
Dura mater — outermost, toughest. Two layers in the cranium (periosteal and meningeal) fused except where they form venous sinuses.
That's cerebrospinal fluid, not a meningeal layer.

questions.q: The limbic system is primarily associated with:
questions.explain: The limbic system — including amygdala, hippocampus, and cingulate gyrus — processes emotions, motivation, and memory formation.

optionExplains.text: Motor control involves motor cortex, basal ganglia, cerebellum. Not limbic.
Limbic system — the emotional brain. Amygdala processes fear, hippocampus forms memories, cingulate gyrus connects emotion to cognition. It's why emotional memories last.
Visual processing is occipital cortex. Limbic system adds emotional meaning to what you see.
Respiration is brainstem territory — medullary centers. Limbic is emotional.

questions.q: Cerebrospinal fluid is produced by the:
questions.explain: Choroid plexuses are specialized capillary networks in the ventricles that produce CSF by filtering blood plasma.

optionExplains.text: Dura mater is protective membrane. It doesn't produce CSF.
Choroid plexuses — clusters of capillaries and ependymal cells in each ventricle. They produce about 500mL of CSF daily, constantly renewed.
Arachnoid villi ABSORB CSF back into venous blood. Production is choroid plexuses.
Pia mater lines brain surface but doesn't produce CSF.

questions.q: The blood-brain barrier is formed primarily by:
questions.explain: The blood-brain barrier is formed by tight junctions between capillary endothelial cells, supported by astrocyte end-feet wrapping around the vessels.

optionExplains.text: Neurons are protected BY the barrier but don't form it.

Astrocytes wrap capillaries with their end-feet, inducing tight junctions in the endothelium. Together they create a selective barrier — keeping pathogens and toxins out while allowing nutrients through.
Oligodendrocytes make CNS myelin. Not involved in blood-brain barrier.
Microglia are immune cells of the CNS. They benefit from the barrier but don't form it.

questions.q: The structure connecting the third and fourth ventricles is the:
questions.explain: The cerebral aqueduct (of Sylvius) is a narrow channel through the midbrain connecting third ventricle to fourth ventricle.

optionExplains.text: Interventricular foramina connect lateral ventricles to third ventricle. Wrong junction.
Cerebral aqueduct — a narrow tube through the midbrain. Blockage here causes obstructive hydrocephalus as CSF backs up in the lateral and third ventricles.
Central canal is in the spinal cord, below the fourth ventricle.
Lateral apertures drain CSF FROM the fourth ventricle to subarachnoid space.

questions.q: Which structure controls the body's circadian rhythms?
questions.explain: The pineal gland secretes melatonin, regulated by light input from the eyes, controlling sleep-wake cycles and circadian rhythms.

optionExplains.text: Thalamus relays sensory information. Not the circadian clock.
Pineal gland — this tiny cone-shaped structure secretes melatonin in darkness, signaling sleep time. It's the biological clock's hormone output station.
Amygdala processes fear and emotion. Not circadian timing.
Hippocampus handles memory formation. Circadian rhythms are pineal.

questions.q: The basal nuclei are primarily involved in:
questions.explain: Basal nuclei (basal ganglia) filter and regulate voluntary movement. They help initiate wanted movements and suppress unwanted ones.

optionExplains.text: Sensory processing is cortical — somatosensory, visual, auditory cortices.
Basal nuclei regulate movement — starting, stopping, and smoothing motor commands. Parkinson's disease damages the substantia nigra (basal nuclei input), causing tremors and rigidity.
Memory formation is hippocampal. Basal nuclei are motor.
Language comprehension is Wernicke's area. Basal nuclei are motor regulation.

questions.q: The primary somatosensory cortex is located in the:
questions.explain: Primary somatosensory cortex occupies the postcentral gyrus — immediately posterior to the central sulcus. It receives touch, pressure, temperature, and pain signals.

optionExplains.text: Precentral gyrus is primary MOTOR cortex — sends commands, doesn't receive sensation.
Postcentral gyrus — the sensory homunculus maps body surface here. Lips and hands get huge representation because they need fine sensory discrimination.
Superior temporal gyrus is primary auditory cortex — hearing, not touch.
Inferior frontal gyrus contains Broca's area — speech, not sensation.

questions.q: The hypothalamus regulates all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explain: Hypothalamus is the homeostatic control center — temperature, hunger, thirst, circadian rhythms, hormones. Voluntary movement is motor cortex and basal ganglia.

optionExplains.text: Hypothalamus controls thermoregulation — your internal thermostat.
Hypothalamus contains hunger and satiety centers controlling eating and drinking.
Voluntary movement is controlled by motor cortex, modulated by basal ganglia and cerebellum. Hypothalamus handles autonomic and endocrine functions, not voluntary motor control.
Hypothalamus links to pineal gland and regulates circadian rhythms.

questions.q: Which cranial fossa contains the cerebellum?
questions.explain: The posterior cranial fossa — deepest and lowest — houses the cerebellum, pons, and medulla. Formed by occipital bone.

optionExplains.text: Anterior fossa holds frontal lobes. Too far forward for cerebellum.
Middle fossa holds temporal lobes and pituitary. Not the cerebellum.
Posterior cranial fossa — the cerebellum fits into this bowl-shaped depression. It's protected by the occipital bone and tentorium cerebelli above.
No superior cranial fossa exists — just anterior, middle, and posterior.

questions.q: The amygdala is primarily responsible for:
questions.explain: The amygdala — almond-shaped nuclei in the temporal lobes — processes fear, aggression, and emotional significance of stimuli.

optionExplains.text: Balance is cerebellar and vestibular. Amygdala is emotional.
Amygdala — the fear center. It tags experiences as emotionally significant, especially threatening ones. Damage reduces fear responses; overstimulation causes anxiety disorders.
Speech production is Broca's area in frontal lobe. Amygdala is emotion.
Visual processing is occipital. Amygdala adds emotional meaning to what you see.

questions.q: The reticular formation is located in the:
questions.explain: The reticular formation is a netlike core of nuclei running through the brainstem, controlling arousal, attention, and sleep-wake transitions.

optionExplains.text: Cortex is surface gray matter. Reticular formation is deep in brainstem.
Reticular formation — scattered through midbrain, pons, and medulla. The reticular activating system (RAS) keeps you awake and alert. Damage causes coma.
Cerebellum coordinates movement. Reticular formation is brainstem arousal.
Thalamus is diencephalon. Reticular formation is brainstem — though they connect.

questions.q: Wernicke's area is located in which lobe?
questions.explain: Wernicke's area is in the left temporal lobe (usually) and processes language comprehension. Damage causes fluent but meaningless speech.

optionExplains.text: Frontal lobe contains Broca's area for speech production, not Wernicke's.
Parietal lobe handles sensory processing. Wernicke's is temporal.
Wernicke's area — posterior temporal lobe, near auditory cortex. It decodes the meaning of heard words. Damage causes Wernicke's aphasia: Fluent speech that makes no sense.
Occipital lobe is visual. Language comprehension is temporal.
